results.possibleIdentity:
Lawrence G. "Larry" Schear was an electrical engineer and computer technology specialist active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in Trenton, New Jersey, he worked for the Western Electric Company, specifically at its Engineering Research Center (ERC) in nearby Princeton. Schear is notably recognized for his collaboration with Claude A. R. Kagan on "The Home Reckoner," a visionary concept for a modular, low-cost home computer system designed to integrate with consumer electronics, such as using audio cassettes for data storage. Their work was published and presented at the 1973 AFIPS National Computer Conference.

Schear was a member of the R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. (Radically Emphatic Students Interested in Science, Technology, and Other Research Studies), an early and influential computer club for young people founded by Kagan in a barn on his property in Hopewell, New Jersey. Through his association with Kagan and the R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S., Schear was part of a technical circle that intersected with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which facilitated collaborations between the engineering community (particularly from Bell Labs and Western Electric) and artists. He attended Drexel University as a co-op student for Western Electric, graduating in 1974, and was an amateur radio enthusiast with the call sign K2EOP.
